analysis: Stronger heuristics (e.g., Heuristic 3, 7, 8, 9) incorporate aggressive dominated-point pruning, vectorized Monte Carlo sampling, and incremental updates to marginal contributions. They reuse shared sample sets and process data in memory‐safe blocks, avoiding redundant hypervolume recomputations. In contrast, weaker ones (e.g., Heuristic 10) tend to recalc hypervolumes repeatedly or use heavier, less optimized local search loops, increasing runtime overhead and limiting scalability.
experience: Focus on efficient pre‐processing (like skyline pruning) and reuse MC samples with vectorized, incremental updates. Limit expensive exact hypervolume calls by confining local swaps to small candidate shortlists, thus balancing quality and speed.
current_reflection: Focus on efficient pre‐processing (like skyline pruning) and reuse MC samples with vectorized, incremental updates. Limit expensive exact hypervolume calls by confining local swaps to small candidate shortlists, thus balancing quality and speed.
bad_reflections: Pre‑prune dominated points, reuse one shared sample set, compute a chunked boolean dominated matrix, and maintain incremental uncovered‑sample counts for greedy picks. Cap sample size adaptively, reserve exact HV only for a tiny shortlist, avoid per‑candidate HV recomputation, prefer vectorized numpy operations, and add deterministic seeds and simple box‑volume tie‑breaks.

Focus on reducing candidate size via pruning, maximize vectorized operations and shared sample reuse, and limit full‐HV recalculations with local swap refinements. Keep memory usage controlled by chunking computations to boost overall runtime efficiency.

Focus on early pruning, vectorized operations, and shared sampling to reduce overhead. Use block-chunking and caching for expensive computations, and integrate small-budget local refinements to polish results efficiently.
comprehensive_reflection:
• Keywords: efficient pre‐processing, skyline pruning, MC sample reuse, vectorized updates, candidate shortlist, local swaps.  
• Advice: Reuse Monte Carlo samples with incremental, vectorized operations and restrict expensive hypervolume calculations to small, high-value candidate sets.  
• Avoid: Full recomputation or sweeping hypervolume evaluations over large candidate pools that slow down the process.  
• Explanation: By pruning dominated points early and leveraging shared sampling, the approach balances quality and speed—using lightweight incremental updates and local refinements to maintain efficiency while reducing computational overhead.
